name: Pietas
description: Pietas, in Roman mythology, embodies the concept of dutifulness, loyalty, and reverence. She represents the profound sense of responsibility one owes to family, country, and the gods. More than simply obedience, Pietas signifies a deep-seated respect and affection that motivates individuals to fulfill their obligations with sincerity and devotion. Her origins are tied to the core Roman values of order, tradition, and the importance of maintaining societal harmony. Pietas' influence extended throughout Roman life, shaping social and political structures. She was not typically associated with specific powers in the magical sense, but rather her strength lay in her ability to inspire virtuous behavior and uphold the moral fabric of Roman society. Temples and altars were dedicated to Pietas, and she was often depicted in art as a woman performing acts of piety, such as caring for her father or offering sacrifices. Emperors often invoked Pietas to legitimize their rule and project an image of virtuous leadership. The concept of Pietas permeated Roman law, religion, and social etiquette, serving as a cornerstone of their civilization. Her role was crucial in maintaining stability and ensuring the continuation of Roman traditions. Pietas was not just a personal virtue but a public one, essential for the well-being of the state. She was seen as the antithesis of neglect, betrayal, and impiety, and her worship was intended to foster a sense of collective responsibility among Roman citizens. Pietas remains a significant concept in understanding the Roman worldview and the values they held most dear.
subtitle: Duty, Loyalty, and Reverence

keyMyths.title: Aeneas and Pietas
keyMyths.description: Aeneas embodies Pietas through his devotion to his father Anchises, his son Ascanius, and his mission to found Rome. He prioritizes his duty over personal desires, demonstrating unwavering commitment.

keyMyths.title: The Daughter's Piety
keyMyths.description: A woman secretly nurses her imprisoned father, condemned to death by starvation, defying the law and exemplifying filial piety. Her devotion moves the authorities to release him.
keyMyths.source: Valerius Maximus, Factorum et Dictorum Memorabilium

keyMyths.title: Cicero and Pietas
keyMyths.description: Cicero often invokes Pietas in his speeches and writings, emphasizing its importance for maintaining social order and upholding the values of the Roman Republic. He views it as essential for good citizenship.

sources.author: Valerius Maximus
sources.description: Collection of historical anecdotes illustrating Roman virtues, including Pietas.
sources.text: Collection of historical anecdotes illustrating Roman virtues, including Pietas.

sources.author: Cicero
sources.description: Speeches and writings emphasizing the importance of Pietas in Roman society.
sources.text: Speeches and writings emphasizing the importance of Pietas in Roman society.
